topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee tasted like ash this morning. I watched Mark from across the crowded breakroom, meticulously organizing his notes on a thick binder. He was wearing a crisp, pale blue shirt, the kind of attire that suggested effortless control. I didn't bother looking away when he glanced my way; there was no recognition in his eyes, just a practiced, neutral assessment. I took a slow sip, letting the bitterness settle. It was just another Tuesday, another routine performance of existing. Then, a voice drifted over from his office, smooth and modulated, "We need to explore the roots of that early conflict, don't you think?" My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. I shifted my weight, the chair scraping slightly against the linoleum floor, and turned back to my lukewarm mug, focusing intently on the swirling brown surface, refusing to engage with the sudden, unwelcome shift in atmosphere.

[story 2]

I walked past the familiar hallway, my pace measured, my shoulders slumped just enough to suggest a profound lack of urgency. Mark was there, sitting in a plush, beige armchair, leaning forward, his expression one of intense, focused empathy. He was talking to a nervous-looking intern, his hands gesturing with a gentle, practiced rhythm. I kept my eyes fixed on the scuff marks on the floor tiles, tracing their patterns with my gaze as if they held all the necessary meaning. When he finally turned, catching my stare, there was a flicker—a brief, almost involuntary tightening around his mouth before he smoothed it back into its usual placid expression. I offered a small, almost nonexistent nod, a gesture that communicated nothing more than acknowledgment, and continued my journey toward the exit, the weight of the encounter settling heavily, yet remaining entirely unmoving within my chest.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights hummed overhead, a constant, irritating drone. I was filing paperwork at my desk, the rhythm of the clicking keyboard the only sound that seemed to hold any real significance in the room. Mark entered, carrying a stack of folders, and paused near my cubicle. He didn't offer a greeting, just a quiet observation, his gaze sweeping over my workspace with an unnerving thoroughness. "You seem... settled," he remarked, his tone devoid of judgment, purely observational. I stopped typing, my fingers hovering over the keys. I looked up slowly, meeting his eyes for a beat too long. There was a strange, hollow space behind my own gaze. I simply offered a slight dip of my chin, a gesture so minimal it might have been mistaken for fatigue, and returned to the screen, the silence between us stretching taut, unyielding.
